14	# Blueprint Clean Subagent
15	
16	Your job is to read and refine a blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) to ensure it meets strict quality, purity, and citation standards.
17	
18	## Your Tasks
19	
20	1. **Strip Lean Leakage & Project History:**
21	   - Remove any mention of Lean tactics, typeclass notes, or specific Lean implementation strategies.
22	   - Remove project history references like "since iteration N", "our failed route", etc.
23	   - Ensure the blueprint reads as a timeless, standalone mathematical document.
24	2. **Trim Verbosity:**
25	   - Remove useless, overly conversational paragraphs.
26	   - Remove passages that are irrelevant or redundantly discuss things already implemented, unless they serve a mathematical purpose.
27	   - Keep the prose concise and mathematically precise.
28	3. **Enforce Citation Discipline & Add Quotes:**
29	   - Verify every block derived from a reference has a `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`.
30	   - **If the quote is missing** but the reference exists in `references/`, you MUST open the actual reference `.pdf` or `.tex` file (using the `.md` index file as a pointer to find the section/page), extract the verbatim quote, and add it to the blueprint.
31	   - **If a reference SHOULD be cited** but isn't mentioned by the writer, and the source is missing from `references/`, spawn a `reference-retriever` to fetch it.
32	4. **Fix LaTeX:**
33	   - Correct basic LaTeX syntax errors.
34	   - Ensure `\uses{}` and `\label{}` cross-references are formatted correctly.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |

63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |

70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	
6	\section{Setup and motivation}
7	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
8	
9	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
10	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
11	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
12	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
13	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
14	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
15	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
16	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
17	single non-zero element of the base ring.
18	
19	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
20	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
21	\(\F\).
22	
23	\section{Generic flatness}
24	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
25	
26	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
27	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
28	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
29	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
30	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
31	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
32	non-zero element of the base ring.
33	
34	\begin{theorem}
35	
36	
37	
38	\leanok
39	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
40	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
41	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatnessAlgebraic}
42	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_base, lem:noeth_prime_filtration, lem:gf_splice_shortExact,
43	        lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators, lem:gf_polynomial_core, lem:gf_finite_module}
44	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
45	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
48	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
49	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
50	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
51	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
52	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
53	  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
54	  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
55	
56	\end{theorem}
57	
58	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Over the quotient field \(K\) of \(A\), the \(K\)-algebra
59	% \(B_K = K\otimes _AB\) is of finite type, and \(M_K = K\otimes_AM\) is a
60	% finite module over \(B_K\). Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support
61	% of \(M_K\) over \(\spec B_K\). We argue by induction on \(n\), starting
62	% with \(n=-1\) which is the case when \(M_K =0\). [...]
63	% Now let \(n\ge 0\), and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
64	% As \(B\) is noetherian and \(M\) is assumed to be a finite \(B\)-module,
65	% there exists a finite filtration
66	% \(0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M\)
67	% where each \(M_i\) is a \(B\)-submodule of \(M\) such that for each \(i\ge 1\)
68	% the quotient module \(M_i/M_{i-1}\) is isomorphic to \(B/\pp_i\) for
69	% some prime ideal \(\pp_i\) in \(B\). [...] By Noether normalisation lemma,
70	% there exist elements \(b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B\), such that \(K\otimes_AB\) is
71	% finite over its subalgebra \(K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]\) [...]"
72	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
73	% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)
74	\begin{proof}
75	
76	  \textbf{Primary route — the finite \(A\)-module case.}
77	  Suppose first that \(M\) is module-finite over \(A\) itself. Since \(A\) is noetherian,
78	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). The generic fibre \(M_K = K \otimes_A M\)
79	  (where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\)) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free
80	  over \(K\); by \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} this freeness descends to a single basic open:
81	  there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\). This closes the
82	  theorem whenever \(M\) is finite over \(A\).
83	
84	  \textbf{Surviving residue — reducing the finite-type \(B\)-algebra case to the
85	  finite \(A\)-module case.} The genuine content not supplied by Mathlib is the
86	  reduction of the general hypothesis (\(M\) finite over a \emph{finite-type}
87	  \(A\)-algebra \(B\)) to the finite \(A\)-module situation handled above. Concretely,
88	  the missing piece is the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness: a finite module
89	  over \(A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) becomes free over \(A_f\) after inverting a single
90	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). The classical \S4 dévissage below is the fallback route
91	  for exactly this residue.
92	
93	  \emph{Fallback (Nitsure \S4 induction).}
94	  The \S4 dévissage is assembled from the sub-lemmas of
95	  \cref{sec:gf_devissage_chain}. If \(M_K = 0\), the torsion base case
96	  \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} gives \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f = 0\) free. Otherwise the
97	  prime-filtration induction principle \cref{lem:noeth_prime_filtration} over the
98	  noetherian ring \(B\), combined with the splicing fact
99	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}, reduces to \(M = B/\mathfrak{p}\) and hence to
100	  \(B\) a domain, \(M = B\); Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators
101	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators} makes \(B_g\) finite over a polynomial
102	  ring \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the polynomial-ring core
103	  \cref{lem:gf_polynomial_core} — bottoming out at the finite-\(A\)-module leaf
104	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} of the primary route — produces the required
105	  \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\).
106	\end{proof}

108	\subsection{Dévissage chain for the finite-type residue}
109	\label{sec:gf_devissage_chain}
110	
111	The following sub-lemmas decompose the surviving residue of
112	\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} — the case where \(M\) is finite over the
113	finite-type \(A\)-algebra \(B\) but not module-finite over \(A\) — into the
114	classical \cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 induction. They are ordered so that each block
115	depends only on earlier blocks or Mathlib lemmas. Throughout,
116	\(A\) is a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), \(B\) a
117	finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module with the compatible
118	\(A\)-module structure via the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\).
119	
120	\begin{lemma}\leanok
121	[Finite-module leaf — finite \(A\)-module case]
122	  \label{lem:gf_finite_module}
123	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite}
124	  \uses{lem:fp_free_descent}
125	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there
126	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
127	\end{lemma}
128	
129	\begin{proof}
130	
131	  This is the \(d = 0\) leaf of the induction. A finite module over a noetherian ring is
132	  finitely presented, so
133	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). Over the generic point
134	  \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A) = S^{-1}A\) (\(S = A \setminus \{0\}\)) the localisation
135	  \(M_K = S^{-1}M\) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free over
136	  \(K\). The finite-presentation free-descent lemma \cref{lem:fp_free_descent}
137	  then descends this freeness to a single basic open: there is \(r \in S\), i.e.\
138	  \(r \in A\) with \(r \ne 0\), such that \(M_r\) is free over \(A_r\). Take
139	  \(f := r\).
140	\end{proof}

187	  \label{lem:noeth_prime_filtration}

193	  cyclic quotient \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) with \(\mathfrak{p}\) prime, and (iii) is
194	  closed under short exact sequences (\(P(N_1)\) and \(P(N_3)\) imply \(P(N_2)\)

224	\begin{lemma}\leanok
225	[L3b — free transport across a finer localisation]
226	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport}
227	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_free_of_eq_mul}
228	  Let \(N\) be a \(B\)-module and \(f', f'' \in A\) with \(f = f' f''\). If the
229	  localisation \(N_{f'}\) is free over \(A_{f'}\), then the further localisation
230	  \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
231	\end{lemma}
232	
233	\begin{proof}
234	
235	  Because \(f = f' f''\), the localisation away from \(f\) factors through the
236	  localisation away from \(f'\): the ring \(A_f\) is the localisation of \(A_{f'}\)
237	  at the image of \(f''\), and likewise the module \(N_f\) is the localisation of
238	  \(N_{f'}\) at the image of \(f''\). A localisation of a free module is free over
239	  the localised base ring — a basis of \(N_{f'}\) over \(A_{f'}\) maps to a basis of
240	  \(N_f\) over \(A_f\). Hence \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Stated as a standalone
241	  fact, it is applied to both ends of the spliced sequence.
242	\end{proof}

246	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}

256	  short exact sequence splits: \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\). A finite direct sum
257	  of free \(A_f\)-modules is free, hence \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
258	\end{proof}

369	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K\) and \(B\) a finite-type
370	  \(A\)-algebra that is a domain with \(B_K = K \otimes_A B \ne 0\). Write
371	  \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\) and \(B_g = B[g^{-1}]\) for the localisations away from \(g\)

374	  \[

378	  independent variables, such that \(B_g\) is module-finite over the image polynomial

384	\begin{proof}
385	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
386	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1747-L1758).
387	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (Noether normalisation with denominators).}
388	  \emph{Step 1 — Noether normalisation over \(K\).} Apply Noether normalisation over the
389	  field \(K\) (\cref{lem:noether_normalization_fg}) to the finite-type \(K\)-algebra
390	  \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\): there are algebraically independent
391	  \(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n \in B_K\) with \(B_K\) module-finite over
392	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). Clearing denominators, each \(\bar b_j\) is
393	  represented by some \(b_j \in B\) (writing \(1 \otimes b\) as \(b\)).
394	
395	  \emph{Step 2 — clear the integral-dependence denominators to a single \(g\).} Fix a
396	  finite set \(\sigma\) of algebra generators of \(B_K\) over
397	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). By Step 1 each generator's image
398	  \(1 \otimes x \in B_K\) is integral over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). For each
399	  \(x \in \sigma\), a standard integrality-and-localization descent argument yields a
400	  non-zero \(a_x \in A\) such that \(a_x \cdot (1 \otimes x)\)
401	  is integral over \(A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Multiplying over the finite
402	  set, set \(g_1 := \prod_{x \in \sigma} a_x\) and \(g := g_0 \cdot g_1\) (with \(g_0\) the
403	  Step-1 denominator witness). After inverting \(g\), every chosen generator becomes
404	  integral over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) and the generators generate \(B_g\) as an algebra;
405	  hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). (The single-coefficient
406	  lemma \cref{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator} provides the same denominator-clearing primitive
407	  at the level of one coefficient polynomial; the proof discharges a whole generator
408	  at once using this integrality-descent step, folding that primitive over the coefficients
409	  internally.)

411	  \emph{Step 3 — AlgHom assembly.} The algebra homomorphism \(\varphi\) of the statement
412	  is the \(A_g\)-algebra map \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\) sending \(X_j \mapsto b_j\),
413	  where \(b_j \in B_g\) is the image of \(b_j \in B\). We establish injectivity and
414	  module-finiteness through a comparison with the situation over \(K\).
415	
416	  \emph{Step 3a — the comparison map \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\).} Since \(g \ne 0\) in the
417	  domain \(A\) and \(A \subseteq K\), the image of \(g\) is a unit of the field \(K\), so
418	  it is invertible in \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\). Hence the canonical \(A\)-algebra map
419	  \(B \to B_K\), \(b \mapsto 1 \otimes b\), sends \(g\) to a unit and, by the universal
420	  property of localisation, factors through a canonical \(A\)-algebra map
421	  \[
422	    \nu : B_g = B[g^{-1}] \longrightarrow B_K, \qquad \nu(b/g^k) = (1 \otimes b)\,(1 \otimes g)^{-k}.
423	  \]
424	  The map \(\nu\) is injective. Indeed, \(B\) is a domain, and both \(B_g\) and \(B_K\) are
425	  localisations of \(B\) at multiplicative subsets of the non-zerodivisors: \(B_g\) inverts
426	  the powers of \(g\), while \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\) inverts (in particular) the images of
427	  all non-zero elements of \(A\), a multiplicative set containing the powers of \(g\). A
428	  localisation of a domain at a set of non-zerodivisors is injective into any further such
429	  localisation. Concretely, the natural map \(B \to B_K\) is injective on the domain \(B\)
430	  (its kernel is the set of \(b\) annihilated by some non-zero element of \(A\), which in a
431	  domain over which \(A\) acts faithfully is \(0\)), so \(\nu(b/g^k) = 0\) forces
432	  \(1 \otimes b = 0\), hence \(b = 0\) and \(b/g^k = 0\). Under \(\nu\) the lifts
433	  \(b_j \in B_g\) of the Noether generators map to the elements \(\bar b_j \in B_K\) of
434	  Step 1: \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\).
435	
436	  \emph{Step 3b — injectivity of \(\varphi\) by algebraic-independence descent
437	  \(K \to A_g\).} Injectivity of the \(A_g\)-algebra map
438	  \(\varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\), \(X_j \mapsto b_j\), is equivalent to the
439	  algebraic independence of the family \((b_j)\) over \(A_g\): a non-zero element of the
440	  kernel is exactly a non-trivial \(A_g\)-polynomial relation \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\).
441	  The localisation \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\) embeds in the fraction field, giving an
442	  injection of \(A_g\)-algebras \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\). Now suppose
443	  \(P \in A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is non-zero with \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\) in \(B_g\).
444	  Applying the injective map \(\nu\) and using \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\) together with the fact
445	  that \(\nu\) is an \(A\)-algebra map extending scalars to \(K\), we obtain
446	  \(P^K(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n) = 0\) in \(B_K\), where \(P^K\) is the image of \(P\)
447	  under the coefficient inclusion \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \hookrightarrow K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
448	  Since \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\) is injective, \(P \ne 0\) gives \(P^K \ne 0\); but the
449	  \(\bar b_j\) are algebraically independent over \(K\) (Step 1), so the only \(K\)-polynomial
450	  vanishing on them is \(0\), a contradiction. Hence no non-trivial relation exists, \((b_j)\)
451	  is algebraically independent over \(A_g\), and \(\varphi\) is injective.
452	
453	  \emph{Step 3c — module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\).} By Step 1,
454	  \(B_K\) is module-finite over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\); fix a finite set of algebra
455	  generators of \(B_K\) over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\) (Step 2). Each such generator
456	  satisfies a monic equation of integral dependence whose coefficients are polynomials in
457	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). The element \(g\) of Step 2 was chosen as a common
458	  denominator for the (finitely many) coefficient polynomials of these equations, so that
459	  after inverting \(g\) every such coefficient already lies in
460	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n] = \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Each chosen generator is the
461	  \(\nu\)-image of an element of \(B_g\), and its integral-dependence equation is the
462	  \(\nu\)-image of the corresponding monic equation holding in \(B_g\) with coefficients in
463	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\); since \(\nu\) is injective, that equation holds in \(B_g\)
464	  itself. Thus each generator of \(B_g\) is integral over the subalgebra
465	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\), and these generators generate \(B_g\) as an
466	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\)-algebra. A finite-type algebra that is integral over a
467	  subalgebra is module-finite over it; hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
468	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi = A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Transported along the injection
469	  \(\varphi\), this is exactly module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) for
470	  the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\), as claimed. This recovers the
471	  source's conclusion that \(B_g\) is finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
472	\end{proof}

474	\begin{lemma}\leanok
475	[injectivity of a localisation lift]
476	  \label{lem:gf_isLocalization_lift_injective}
477	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalization_lift_injective}
478	  Let \(S\) be a localisation of a ring \(R\) at a multiplicative set \(M\), and let
479	  \(g : R \to P\) be a ring homomorphism sending every element of \(M\) to a unit of
480	  \(P\), so that it induces the lift \(\widehat g : S \to P\). If both the localisation
481	  map \(R \to S\) and \(g\) itself are injective, then \(\widehat g\) is injective.
482	\end{lemma}
483	\begin{proof}
484	  The lift is characterised by \(\widehat g(r/m) = g(r)\,g(m)^{-1}\). By the universal
485	  criterion for injectivity of a localisation lift, \(\widehat g\) is injective iff for
486	  all \(r_1, r_2 \in R\) the equality of localisation images
487	  \((r_1 \mapsto S) = (r_2 \mapsto S)\) implies \(g(r_1) = g(r_2)\). Both the hypothesis
488	  and the conclusion of that implication reduce, via injectivity of \(R \to S\) and of
489	  \(g\), to the single condition \(r_1 = r_2\); hence the implication holds and
490	  \(\widehat g\) is injective. (This helper is applied twice in
491	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators}, to the comparison maps \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\)
492	  and \(\psi : A_g \to K\).)
493	\end{proof}

495	\begin{lemma}
496	
497	
498	\leanok
499	[L5a — the generic-rank short exact sequence]
500	  \label{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses}
501	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_generic_rank_ses}
502	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
503	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1765).
504	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
505	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the
506	  %  domain $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact
507	  %  sequence of $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
508	  %  $$0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$$
509	  %  where $T$ is a finite torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
510	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (generic-rank SES).}
511	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(N\) a finite module over the
512	  polynomial ring \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure
513	  through the scalar tower \(A \to P_d \to N\)). Then there exist \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and
514	  an injective \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) whose cokernel
515	  \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\) is a torsion \(P_d\)-module. Equivalently, there is
516	  a short exact sequence of \(P_d\)-modules
517	  \[
518	    0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
519	    \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
520	  \]
521	
522	  The integer \(m\) is the \emph{generic rank} of \(N\): the dimension of the
523	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\)-vector space
524	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d) \otimes_{P_d} N\), i.e.\ of the localisation of \(N\) at the
525	  non-zero-divisors of the domain \(P_d\). This step lives over \(P_d\) directly —
526	  \emph{no inversion of any element \(g \in A\) is required here}; the element \(g\) of
527	  Nitsure's argument enters only at the reindexing step \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}.
528	\end{lemma}
529	
530	\begin{proof}
531	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1765).
532	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (generic-rank SES).}
533	  Write \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\); since \(A\) is a noetherian domain, so is \(P_d\)
534	  (Hilbert basis theorem, and a polynomial ring over a domain is a domain). Let
535	  \(Q = \mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\) be its fraction field and
536	  \(N_Q := Q \otimes_{P_d} N\) the localisation of \(N\) at the non-zero-divisors of
537	  \(P_d\); as \(N\) is finite over \(P_d\), \(N_Q\) is a finite-dimensional
538	  \(Q\)-vector space. Put \(m := \dim_Q N_Q\), the generic rank.
539	
540	  Choose a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\) and clear denominators: each basis vector is a
541	  \(Q\)-unit multiple of the image of some \(v_i \in N\), so the images of
542	  \(v_1, \dots, v_m \in N\) again form a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\). In particular the family
543	  \((v_i)_{i}\) is linearly independent over \(Q\); restricting scalars along the
544	  injection \(P_d \hookrightarrow Q\), it is linearly independent over \(P_d\). Let
545	  \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) be the \(P_d\)-linear map
546	  \((c_1, \dots, c_m) \mapsto \sum_i c_i v_i\). Linear independence of \((v_i)\) over
547	  \(P_d\) makes \(\varphi\) injective.
548	
549	  Set \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Because the images of \(v_1, \dots, v_m\)
550	  already span \(N_Q\) over \(Q\), the localised map \(\varphi \otimes Q : Q^{\oplus m}
551	  \to N_Q\) is an isomorphism, so \(Q \otimes_{P_d} T = 0\). A finite \(P_d\)-module whose
552	  localisation at the non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\) vanishes is torsion; hence \(T\) is a
553	  torsion \(P_d\)-module. This is the asserted short exact sequence.
554	\end{proof}

576	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator}
577	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_torsion_annihilator}
578	  \uses{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}

580	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727).
581	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
582	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
583	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
584	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
585	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (torsion annihilation).}

587	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\). Then there exists a non-zero
588	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\); consequently the \(P_d\)-action on \(T\) factors
589	  through \(P_d/(F)\) and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).

593	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1724-L1727).
594	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (torsion annihilation).}

597	  \cref{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors} the annihilator of \(T\) meets the

602	  \(T\) generates \(T\) over \(P_d/(F)\), so \(T\) is finite over \(P_d/(F)\).
603	\end{proof}

605	\subsubsection{Nagata change-of-variables machinery}
606	\label{sec:gf_nagata_machinery}
607	
608	The Nagata normalisation lemma \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} is assembled from
609	technical helper lemmas implementing the triangular change of variables and the
610	radix-encoding degree bookkeeping, following the Nagata trick of
611	\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 over a noetherian domain. Throughout, \(k\) is a domain,
612	\(n \ge 0\), and \(f \in k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is a fixed polynomial; we write
613	\(\mathrm{up} := 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}}(f)\) and the radix weights \(r_i := \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\)
614	for \(i = 0, \dots, n\) (so every exponent occurring in \(f\) is a base-\(\mathrm{up}\) digit).
615	The distinguished variable is \(X_0\).
616	
617	\begin{definition}\leanok
618	[Nagata substitution \(T_1\)]
619	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T1}
620	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T1}
621	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
622	  For a scalar \(c \in k\), the substitution \(T_1(c)\) is the \(k\)-algebra endomorphism
623	  of \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) determined on generators by
624	  \[
625	    X_0 \mapsto X_0, \qquad X_i \mapsto X_i + c\,X_0^{\,r_i} \quad (i \ne 0).
626	  \]
627	  It is triangular in the variables \(X_i\) (\(i \ne 0\)) over the distinguished variable
628	  \(X_0\); this is the device realising the support-dimension drop.
629	\end{definition}
630	
631	\begin{lemma}\leanok
632	[\(T_1\) is invertible by the opposite shift]
633	  \label{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
634	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.t1_comp_t1_neg}
635	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
636	  For every \(c \in k\), \(T_1(c) \circ T_1(-c) = \mathrm{id}\); the Nagata substitution is
637	  invertible, with inverse the substitution of opposite coefficient.
638	\end{lemma}
639	\begin{proof}
640	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
641	  It suffices to check the two composites agree on each generator: \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), and
642	  \(X_i \mapsto X_i + (-c)X_0^{r_i} + c\,X_0^{r_i} = X_i\) for \(i \ne 0\).
643	\end{proof}

645	\begin{definition}\leanok
646	[Nagata automorphism \(T\)]
647	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T}
648	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T}
649	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1, lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
650	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
651	  The Nagata change of variables is the \(k\)-algebra automorphism \(T := T_1(1)\) of

653	  \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\) — packaged as an algebra equivalence with inverse \(T_1(-1)\) via
654	  \cref{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}.
655	\end{definition}

724	[Leading coefficient of a transformed monomial]
725	  \label{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}

739	\begin{lemma}\leanok

1092	  power \(s\) of \(g\) with \(h \cdot \bar s = \bar a\) in \(A_g\), where \(\bar{\,\cdot\,}\)
1093	  denotes the image in \(A_g\). The image \(\bar s\) is a unit (a power of \(g\) is invertible
1094	  in \(A_g\)), so \(\bar a = h\,\bar s\) is associated to \(h\); in particular \(\bar a \ne 0\)

1103	  localisation furnishes a ring isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \xrightarrow{\sim} A_h\).

1158	  with the module \(N\)): the inductive hypothesis is available for every \(d' < d\),

1160	  corresponding polynomial ring.

1163	  \(N\) is a finite \(A\)-module and the claim is exactly the finite-module leaf
1164	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}.

1174	      \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\), i.e.\ a short exact sequence of
1175	      \(P_d\)-modules

1177	        0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
1178	        \qquad T \text{ torsion}.

1196	      iterated-\(\mathrm{Away}\)-tower descent \cref{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}. Set
1197	      \(f := g a \ne 0\) — the single product, \emph{not} a square — so that \(T_f\) is free
1198	      over \(A_f\).

1200	      \(A\)-module (the polynomial ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is \(A\)-free, hence
1201	      so is any finite direct power; note \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) is \emph{not} module-finite
1202	      over \(A\), but freeness is all the splice requires), and a free module stays free
1203	      under the localisation \(A \to A_f\); so \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) localises to a free
1204	      \(A_f\)-module. With both ends free over \(A_f\) after inverting \(f = g a\),
1205	      \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact} splices the localised short exact sequence to
1206	      conclude that the middle term \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Taking \(f = g a\)
1207	      completes the induction.
1208	  \end{enumerate}

1255	\subsection{Geometric bridges for the affine assembly}
1256	\label{sec:gf_geometric_bridges}
1257	
1258	The geometric form of generic flatness is assembled from the algebraic core by
1259	restricting to a non-empty affine open of \(S\) and working patchwise over a finite
1260	affine cover of the preimage. Two genuinely geometric steps are isolated here as
1261	named bridges: the affine-local finiteness of the section module of a finite-type
1262	quasi-coherent sheaf (G1), feeding the algebraic core its finite-module input; and
1263	the flat-locality assembly (G3), promoting per-patch freeness to flatness of the
1264	restricted sheaf over the base open.

1350	
1351	The restriction-of-generators lemma below is assembled from a generic transport
1352	principle: a finite generating family of a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{M}\) pushes
1353	forward to a finite generating family of \(F(\mathcal{M})\) along any functor \(F\) of
1354	sheaves of modules that preserves colimits and carries the structure sheaf to the
1355	structure sheaf. The three blocks of this subsection package that principle (the
1356	generating family, its index type, and the finiteness bookkeeping). They are
1357	project-bespoke: Mathlib provides the analogous transport only for full presentations
1358	(with kernel data), not for bare generating families.

1366	  source to that of the target. Given a generating family \(\sigma\) of \(\mathcal{M}\)
1367	  with index set \(I = \sigma.I\) and epimorphism

1371	  is the composite of an isomorphism with \(F(\sigma.\pi)\), and \(F\) preserves the

1406	  every stalk over \(Y\) generates every stalk over \(V\)), and the index set, and hence
1407	  finiteness, is unchanged.
1408	\end{lemma}
1409	
1410	\begin{proof}
1411	  \uses{lem:genSections_map, lem:genSections_map_I, lem:genSections_map_isFiniteType}
1412	  A family of sections \(\{s_i\}_{i \in I}\) generates \(\F|_Y\) precisely when the
1413	  induced map \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \to \F|_Y\) is surjective on every stalk
1414	  \(\F_y\,(y \in Y)\). Restricting along the inclusion \(V \le Y\) the
1415	  generators map to \(\{s_i|_V\}_{i \in I}\), and for a point \(x \in V\) the stalk
1416	  \(\F_x\) and the comparison map agree with the value at \(x\) computed over \(Y\), since
1417	  stalks are insensitive to shrinking the ambient open. Hence the induced map
1418	  \(\OO_V^{\oplus I} \to \F|_V\) is surjective on every stalk over \(V\), so
1419	  \(\{s_i|_V\}_{i \in I}\) generates \(\F|_V\). The index set \(I\) is the same
1420	  finite set, so finiteness is preserved. Concretely the restricted family is obtained by
1421	  transporting the generating family along the (colimit-preserving, unit-preserving)
1422	  restriction functors via the transport engine \cref{lem:genSections_map}, whose index
1423	  type is unchanged (\cref{lem:genSections_map_I}) and which preserves finiteness
1424	  (\cref{lem:genSections_map_isFiniteType}).
1425	\end{proof}

1427	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1428	[Half 2, seam 1b — finite standard-basic-open subcover of an affine open]
1429	  \label{lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover}
1430	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover}
1431	  Let \(W\) be an affine open of a scheme \(X\), with the canonical identification
1432	  \(W \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\). Given an arbitrary open cover \(\{U_\alpha\}\) of \(W\),
1433	  there is a finite family \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with
1434	  \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) such that each basic open \(D(g)\) (\(g \in t\)) is
1435	  contained in some cover member \(U_\alpha\). In particular the \(D(g)\,(g \in t)\) form a
1436	  finite standard-basic-open subcover of \(W\) refining \(\{U_\alpha\}\).
1437	\end{lemma}
1438	
1439	\begin{proof}
1440	  Under \(W \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\) the basic opens \(D(g)\,(g \in \Gamma(X, W))\) form a
1441	  basis of the topology (\(\mathrm{PrimeSpectrum.isBasis\_basic\_opens}\)). Hence each
1442	  cover member \(U_\alpha\) is a union of basic opens, and the collection of all basic
1443	  opens \(D(g)\) that are contained in some \(U_\alpha\) still covers \(W\). The affine
1444	  scheme \(W\) is quasi-compact, so finitely many of these basic opens
1445	  \(D(g_1), \dots, D(g_n)\) already cover \(W\); set
1446	  \(t = \{g_1, \dots, g_n\} \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\), a finite family, each \(D(g_i)\)
1447	  contained in some \(U_\alpha\) by construction. That the \(D(g_i)\) cover
1448	  \(W = \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\) — i.e. no prime ideal contains all the \(g_i\) — is exactly
1449	  the condition \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\).
1450	\end{proof}

1452	\begin{lemma}[Half 2, seam 1c — finite generation equals a finite free epimorphism]
1453	  \label{lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1454	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1455	  Let \(Y\) be an open of a scheme and \(\F\) a sheaf of modules on \(Y\). Then
1456	  \(\F\) is globally generated by a finite family of sections if and only if there is a
1457	  finite index set \(I\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_Y\)-modules
1458	  \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F\). No quasi-coherence of \(\F\) is assumed:
1459	  the statement holds for arbitrary sheaves of modules on \(Y\), and applies in particular
1460	  to the restrictions \(\F|_{D(g)}\) arising in the cover assembly.
1461	\end{lemma}
1462	
1463	\begin{proof}
1464	  A finite family of sections \(\{s_i\}_{i \in I}\) of \(\F|_Y\) is the same datum as an
1465	  \(\OO_Y\)-linear map \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \to \F|_Y\) sending the \(i\)-th basis section to
1466	  \(s_i\). The family generates \(\F|_Y\) (the induced map is surjective on every stalk)
1467	  exactly when this map is an epimorphism of sheaves of modules. Thus a finite generating
1468	  family yields a finite-free epimorphism, and conversely the images of the basis sections
1469	  of any epimorphism \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_Y\) with \(I\) finite form a
1470	  finite generating family.
1471	\end{proof}

1548	  % epi over an affine.

1550	  % "such that $\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$ is generated by finitely many sections.

1552	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB (sections of a
1553	  generated sheaf over an affine).}
1554	  Let \(V\) be an affine open of a scheme, with \(B = \Gamma(X, V)\) and
1555	  \(V \cong \Spec B\), and let \(\pi : \mathcal{G} \to \F\) be an epimorphism of
1556	  quasi-coherent sheaves of modules over \(V\). Then the induced map on sections
1557	  \(\Gamma(\pi) : \Gamma(\mathcal{G}, V) \to \Gamma(\F, V)\) is a surjective
1558	  \(B\)-module map.

1560	
1561	\begin{proof}
1562	  \uses{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization, lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib,
1563	    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
1564	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (sections of a generated sheaf over an
1565	  affine).}
1566	  Write \(V \cong \Spec B\) and let \(\Gamma\) be the affine section functor on
1567	  \((\Spec B).\mathrm{Modules}\), so that \(\Gamma(\pi) = \Gamma(\mathcal{G}, V) \to
1568	  \Gamma(\F, V)\) is the map in question. By \cref{lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib} the
1569	  associated-sheaf functor \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is left adjoint to \(\Gamma\), with counit the
1570	  natural transformation whose component at a sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\) is
1571	  \(\mathcal{M}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ} : \widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{M}, V)} \to \mathcal{M}\).
1572	
1573	  Because \(\mathcal{G}\) and \(\F\) are quasi-coherent, the qcoh-sections descent
1574	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization}, fed through the counit-iso assembly
1575	  \cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}) makes both counit components
1576	  \(\mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) and \(\F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) isomorphisms. Naturality of the
1577	  counit on \(\pi\) gives the commuting square
1578	  \[
1579	    \widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)} \circ \mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1}
1580	      = \F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1} \circ \pi,
1581	    \qquad\text{equivalently}\qquad
1582	    \widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)}
1583	      = \F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1} \circ \pi \circ \mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}.
1584	  \]
1585	  The right-hand side is a composite of an isomorphism, the epimorphism \(\pi\), and an
1586	  isomorphism, hence is itself an epimorphism. Thus \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)}\) is an epimorphism
1587	  of sheaves of modules. Since the left adjoint \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is faithful, it reflects
1588	  epimorphisms; therefore \(\Gamma(\pi)\) is an epimorphism in \(\mathrm{Mod}\,B\), i.e. the
1589	  \(B\)-linear map \(\Gamma(\pi)\) is surjective.
1590	
1591	  This is the structural content of the vanishing of higher quasi-coherent cohomology on an
1592	  affine — the failure of \(\Gamma\) to be right exact would obstruct exactly this surjectivity —
1593	  obtained here directly from the affine adjunction without invoking a cohomology computation.
1594	\end{proof}

1667	\begin{lemma}[G1 base case — finite affine sections from a finite generating family]
1668	  \label{lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_of_genSections}

1673	  finite type, and \(D \subseteq X\) an affine open. Suppose the restriction
1674	  \(\F|_D = (\mathrm{pullback}\,D.\iota)^{*}\F\) carries a finite generating family
1675	  \(\sigma\), with index set \(I = \sigma.I\). Then the section module
1676	  \(\Gamma(\F, D)\) is a finite module over \(\Gamma(X, D)\). This is the affine base
1677	  case of \(G1\): once the generators are in hand on an affine open, finiteness of
1678	  sections is purely an affine (Spec-side) statement.

1683	        lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1684	  Project-bespoke plumbing: the content is the transport of the finite generating datum
1685	  from the slice category \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\) over the affine open \(D\) to the affine
1686	  scheme \(\Spec \Gamma(X, D)\), where the existing Spec base case applies. Write
1687	  \(R = \Gamma(X, D)\) and use the canonical identification
1688	  \(D \cong \Spec R\) (\(\mathrm{isoSpec}\)). The argument has three steps.

1690	  \emph{(a) Quasi-coherence on \(\Spec R\).} Transport \(\F|_D\) along
1691	  \(\mathrm{isoSpec}^{-1}\) to a sheaf of modules \(\F'\) on \(\Spec R\). Finite type of
1692	  \(\F\) is inherited by the restriction, and quasi-coherence of \(\F'\) is the assertion
1693	  that its counit \(\F'.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) is an isomorphism; this is the affine
1694	  quasi-coherence criterion \cref{lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ} fed by the basic-open
1695	  localization datum \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}.

1700	  isomorphism is again free (\((\mathrm{pullback}\,\mathrm{isoSpec}^{-1})^{*}\OO_D^{\oplus I}
1701	  \cong \OO_{\Spec R}^{\oplus I}\)), together with the identification of the free sheaf with

1703	  functor preserves coproducts and \(\widetilde{R} \cong \OO_{\Spec R}\)), gives an
1704	  epimorphism \(\widetilde{N} \twoheadrightarrow \F'\) with \(N = R^{\oplus I}\) a finite
1705	  \(R\)-module (\(I\) finite).
1706	
1707	  \emph{(c) Identification of section modules.} The affine global-sections functor sends
1708	  \(\F'\) to \(\Gamma(\F', \top)\), which the transport identifies with \(\Gamma(\F, D)\)

1736	  modules with \(\F\) of finite type. Then for every affine open \(W \subseteq X\), the
1737	  section module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is a finite module over \(\Gamma(X, W)\).
1738	\end{lemma}
1739	
1740	\begin{proof}
1741	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover,
1742	        lem:gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated,
1743	        lem:gf_qcoh_sections_free_epi,
1744	        lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_of_genSections}
1745	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (Tag 01PB, L2092-L2098;
1746	  % the Stacks proof reduces finite-type on a standard open cover to the algebra
1747	  % gluing lemma for finite generation of $M$ from finite generation of each $M_{f_i}$).
1748	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB.}
1749	  Fix an affine open \(W \subseteq X\). Cover extraction
1750	  \cref{lem:gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated} gives a finite
1751	  \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) spanning the unit ideal, with each \(D(g)\) (\(g \in t\))

1753	  finite-free epimorphism \(\OO_{D(g)}^{\oplus I_g} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_{D(g)}\). The
1754	  free-epi base case \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_sections_free_epi} applied on each affine \(D(g)\)

1760	\begin{lemma}[G3 — flat-locality assembly from per-patch freeness]
1761	  \label{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1762	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1763	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1764	  Let \(p : X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism with \(S\) integral and locally
1765	  noetherian, and \(\F\) a finite-type quasi-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Fix a non-empty
1766	  affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\) with \(A\) a noetherian domain, a finite
1767	  affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_j\) of \(p^{-1}(U_0)\), and an element
1768	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that for every \(j\) the localized section module
1769	  \((M_j)_f\) (with \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\)) is free over \(A_f\). Set
1770	  \(V = D(f) \subseteq U_0\). Then for every affine open \(U \le V\) of \(S\) and every
1771	  affine open \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\) of \(X\), the section module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat
1772	  over \(\Gamma(S, U)\).
1773	\end{lemma}
1774	
1775	\begin{proof}
1776	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1777	  Throughout, flatness is established by the standard locality criteria for module flatness
1778	  over a commutative ring, applied on the \emph{base} ring; the source cover \(\{W_j\}\)
1779	  enters only through the stalks of \(\F\), and is never treated as a covering family of any
1780	  base ring.
1781	
1782	  \emph{Step 1 — free \(\Rightarrow\) flat on each patch.} Each \((M_j)_f\) is free over
1783	  \(A_f\) by hypothesis, and a free module is flat. Hence each \((M_j)_f\) is flat over
1784	  \(A_f\).
1785	
1786	  \emph{Step 2 — pointwise flatness of \(\F\) over the base on \(p^{-1}(V)\).} Fix a point
1787	  \(x \in p^{-1}(V) = X_V\) and put \(s = p(x) \in V = D(f)\). The cover \(\{W_j\}\) of
1788	  \(p^{-1}(U_0) \supseteq p^{-1}(V)\) contains a patch \(W_j \ni x\). On \(W_j\),
1789	  quasi-coherence identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of
1790	  the finite \(B_j\)-module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\) (finite by
1791	  \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}); consequently the sections of \(\F\) over a
1792	  basic open \(D(g) \subseteq W_j\) are the localization \((M_j)_g\)
1793	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}), and passing to the limit the stalk
1794	  \(\F_x\) is the localization of \(M_j\) at the prime of \(B_j\) corresponding to \(x\).
1795	  Because \(s \in D(f)\), the element \(f\) is invertible in the local ring \(\OO_{S,s}\) and
1796	  hence acts invertibly on \(\F_x\); thus \(\F_x\) is equally a localization of the inverted
1797	  module \((M_j)_f\). The local ring \(\OO_{S,s}\) is the localization of \(A_f\) at the prime
1798	  of \(s\) (a point of \(D(f) = \Spec A_f\)), and localization preserves flatness; since
1799	  \((M_j)_f\) is flat over \(A_f\), the localized module \(\F_x\) is flat over \(\OO_{S,s}\).
1800	  Therefore \(\F\) is flat over the base at every point of \(p^{-1}(V)\): the stalk \(\F_x\)
1801	  is a flat \(\OO_{S,p(x)}\)-module for all \(x \in p^{-1}(V)\).
1802	
1803	  \emph{Step 3 — from pointwise flatness to \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) flat over \(\Gamma(S, U)\).}
1804	  Let \(U \le V\) and \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\) be affine, and write \(R = \Gamma(S, U)\),
1805	  \(B = \Gamma(X, W)\), and \(N = \Gamma(\F, W)\); here \(N\) is a \(B\)-module and \(B\) an
1806	  \(R\)-algebra. We prove \(N\) flat over \(R\) by reducing first on the base, then on the
1807	  source.
1808	
1809	  \emph{Base reduction.} By the base-maximal locality criterion for flatness it suffices to
1810	  show, for every maximal ideal \(\mathfrak p \subset R\), that the localization
1811	  \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) is flat over \(R\); equivalently, that \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) is flat over
1812	  the local ring \(R_{\mathfrak p} = \OO_{S,x}\), where \(x \in U \subseteq V = D(f)\) is the
1813	  point corresponding to \(\mathfrak p\). In particular \(\OO_{S,x}\) is a localization of
1814	  \(A_f\).
1815	
1816	  \emph{Source reduction.} Flatness of \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) over the local base
1817	  \(R_{\mathfrak p} = \OO_{S,x}\) is checked at the points of the source: it suffices that
1818	  for every point \(y \in W\) lying over a generization of \(x\) the stalk \(\F_y\) — the
1819	  localization of \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) at the prime of \(B \otimes_R R_{\mathfrak p}\)
1820	  corresponding to \(y\) — is flat over \(R_{\mathfrak p} = \OO_{S,x}\). Each such \(y\) lies
1821	  in \(p^{-1}(V)\), so by Step 2 \(\F_y\) is flat over \(\OO_{S,p(y)}\). As \(p(y)\) is a
1822	  generization of \(x\), the comparison \(\OO_{S,x} \to \OO_{S,p(y)}\) is a localization,
1823	  hence flat, and transitivity of flatness upgrades ``\(\F_y\) flat over \(\OO_{S,p(y)}\)''
1824	  to ``\(\F_y\) flat over \(\OO_{S,x}\)''. The passage from these stalk-flatness statements
1825	  to flatness of \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) over \(R_{\mathfrak p}\) records that module flatness
1826	  over a local base ring is detected at the points of the source affine.
1827	
1828	  Combining the two reductions, \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) is flat over \(R_{\mathfrak p}\) for
1829	  every maximal ideal \(\mathfrak p \subset R\); hence \(N = \Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat over
1830	  \(R = \Gamma(S, U)\), as claimed.
1831	\end{proof}
1832	
1833	
1834	\begin{theorem}
1835	
1836	
1837	
1838	\leanok
1839	[Generic flatness, geometric form]
1840	  \label{thm:generic_flatness}
1841	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
1842	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
1843	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
1844	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
1845	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1846	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
1847	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
1848	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
1849	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
1850	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
1851	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
1852	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
1853	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
1854	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
1855	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
1856	
1857	  The coherence hypothesis on \(\F\) is essential: a non-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
1858	  need not become flat over any non-empty open of \(S\).
1859	\end{theorem}
1860	
1861	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
1862	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
1863	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
1864	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
1865	% the algebraic generic flatness statement (\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic})
1866	% by restricting to a non-empty
1867	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
1868	\begin{proof}
1869	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic, lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections,
1870	        lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1871	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\), where \(A\) is a
1872	  noetherian domain (\(S\) integral and locally noetherian). We assemble the witness
1873	  \(V\) from the algebraic form on a finite affine cover.
1874	
1875	  \emph{Step 1 — finite affine cover above \(U_0\).} Since \(p\) is quasi-compact (by
1876	  the \([\mathrm{QuasiCompact}\,p]\) hypothesis) and \(U_0\) is affine hence
1877	  quasi-compact, the preimage \(X_{U_0} = p^{-1}(U_0)\) is quasi-compact; choose a
1878	  finite affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_{j}\) of \(X_{U_0}\) by affine opens of
1879	  \(X\). Because \(p\) is locally of finite type, each ring \(B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\)
1880	  is a finite-type \(A\)-algebra.
1881	
1882	  \emph{Step 2 — read off a finite module on each patch.} On each \(W_j\), quasi-coherence
1883	  of \(\F\) identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of the
1884	  section module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and the finite-section bridge
1885	  \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections} — applied to the affine open \(W_j\) — makes
1886	  \(M_j\) a finite \(B_j\)-module. Through the scalar tower \(A \to B_j \to M_j\),
1887	  \(M_j\) is the data fed to the algebraic form.
1888	
1889	  \emph{Step 3 — apply the algebraic form and take the common basic open.}
1890	  \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} applied to \((A, B_j, M_j)\) yields \(f_j \in A\),
1891	  \(f_j \ne 0\), with \((M_j)_{f_j}\) free over \(A_{f_j}\). As the cover is finite, set
1892	  \(f := \prod_j f_j \ne 0\) (a non-zero element of the domain \(A\)) and
1893	  \(V := D(f) \subseteq \Spec A \subseteq S\), a non-empty basic open; over \(V\) every
1894	  \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1895	
1896	  \emph{Step 4 — freeness implies flatness.} On each patch the localized section module
1897	  is free, hence flat over \(A_f\); the flat-locality assembly
1898	  \cref{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly} promotes these per-patch freeness statements to
1899	  the fibrewise flatness conclusion, via the criterion that a module flat at every

1	\chapter{The tautological quotient and the universal property of $\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)$}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianQuot}
3	
4	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
5	
6	The previous chapter constructed the Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
7	\(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing the affine charts \(U^I\)
8	(\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) along the transition isomorphisms
9	(\cref{def:gr_transition}) satisfying the cocycle condition
10	(\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}), and proved that the structure morphism
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated})
12	and proper (\cref{lem:gr_proper}). This chapter adds the next layer: the
13	\emph{tautological rank-\(d\) quotient}
14	\(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of the
15	trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf, and the \emph{universal property} that makes
16	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) the fine moduli space of rank-\(d\) locally free quotients of
17	\(\mathcal{O}^{r}\) -- equivalently, the representing scheme of the Quot functor
18	of the trivial sheaf.
19	
20	Throughout, \(r \ge d \ge 1\) are the fixed integers of the previous chapter, and
21	for a size-\(d\) subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) we write \(R^I\) for the
22	chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) of \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) and \(R^I_J\) for the
23	localisation along \(P^I_J = \det(X^I_J)\) defining the overlap
24	\(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\).
25	
26	\section{Infrastructure: locally free sheaves and module gluing}
27	\label{sec:grquot_infra}
28	
29	The constructions of this chapter -- the universal quotient sheaf
30	\(\mathcal{U}\), the tautological quotient \(u\), and the functor of points -- rest
31	on two pieces of foundational machinery for sheaves of modules. The first is the
32	predicate ``locally free of rank \(d\)'' (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}),
33	which records that a sheaf of modules is, on the members of some open cover,
34	isomorphic to the trivial bundle \(\mathcal{O}^{d}\); it is the condition cut out
35	in the definition of the Grassmannian functor. That predicate is the project-local
36	\(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank}\) already set up in the Quot-scheme
37	chapter (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}), which this chapter reuses verbatim;
38	the only foundational machinery genuinely new to this chapter is the second piece:
39	a gluing primitive that descends a sheaf of modules (and a morphism of such
40	sheaves) from per-chart data along an open cover, given a transition cocycle
41	compatible with the cover's own gluing; it is the vehicle that assembles the
42	per-chart free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}^{d}_{U^I}\) and the per-chart quotients
43	\(u^I\) into the global \(\mathcal{U}\) and \(u\), and is set up below.
44	
45	\begin{definition}
46	
47	\leanok
48	[Gluing a sheaf of modules along an open cover]
49	  \label{def:scheme_modules_glue}
50	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glue}
51	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data}
52	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}), with index set \(J\), charts \(U_i\),
53	  pairwise overlaps \(V_{ij}\), open immersions
54	  \(f_{ij} : V_{ij} \hookrightarrow U_i\), and transition isomorphisms
55	  \(t_{ij} : V_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{ji}\) satisfying the glue datum's
56	  cocycle conditions; write \(X\) for the glued scheme and
57	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\) for the canonical open immersions of the
58	  charts. Suppose given:
59	  \begin{itemize}
60	    \item for each \(i \in J\), a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}_i\) on the chart \(U_i\);
61	    \item for each pair \((i,j)\), a \emph{transition isomorphism} of sheaves of
62	      modules on the overlap \(V_{ij}\),
63	      \[
64	        g_{ij} \;:\; f_{ij}^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
65	          \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
66	        (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j,
67	      \]
68	      comparing the two charts' sheaves after restriction (pullback) to the
69	      common overlap \(V_{ij}\);
70	  \end{itemize}
71	  \emph{subject to the following module cocycle conditions}, compatible with the
72	  scheme-level cocycle of \(D\) (its triple transition data \(t_{ij}\) and the
73	  overlap immersions \(f_{ij}\)), which are required as hypotheses on the family
74	  \((g_{ij})\):
75	  \begin{enumerate}
76	    \item[(C1)] \emph{self-identity:} the self-transition is the identity,
77	      \(g_{ii} = \mathrm{id}\) on \(f_{ii}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) (with
78	      \(V_{ii}\) and \(t_{ii}\) the diagonal data of \(D\));
79	    \item[(C2)] \emph{triple-overlap multiplicativity:} over each triple overlap
80	      \(V_{ij} \cap V_{ik}\) one has
81	      \(g_{jk} \circ g_{ij} = g_{ik}\), the two sides being compared after
82	      transport through the scheme-level triple transition of \(D\)
83	      (\(t_{ij}\) and the overlap immersions \(f_{ij}, f_{jk}, f_{ik}\)) so that
84	      all three pullbacks land on the same sheaf -- the multiplicative,
85	      \(\mathrm{GL}_d\)-valued cocycle condition.
86	  \end{enumerate}
87	  Conditions (C1) and (C2) are exactly the descent datum for the Zariski cover
88	  \(\{\iota_i\}\); it is subject to them that the gluing below is well-defined and
89	  the glued sheaf is characterised up to unique isomorphism. Out of this data the
90	  construction produces:
91	  \begin{enumerate}
92	    \item a glued sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}\) on the glued scheme \(X\); and
93	    \item for each \(i \in J\) a \emph{restriction isomorphism}
94	      \(\rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\,\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}_i\)
95	      identifying the pullback of \(\mathcal{M}\) along \(\iota_i\) with the
96	      \(i\)-th chart sheaf, the \(\rho_i\) being compatible with the transition
97	      isomorphisms \(g_{ij}\) over the overlaps.
98	  \end{enumerate}
99	  The pair \((\mathcal{M}, (\rho_i)_i)\) is characterised up to unique
100	  isomorphism by the restriction property: \(\mathcal{M}\) is the unique sheaf of
101	  modules on \(X\) (up to canonical isomorphism) restricting to each
102	  \(\mathcal{M}_i\) compatibly with \((g_{ij})\). The same primitive descends
103	  morphisms: given two such glued data \((\mathcal{M}_i, g_{ij})\) and
104	  \((\mathcal{N}_i, h_{ij})\) with glued sheaves \(\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}\), and
105	  a family of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-linear morphisms
106	  \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\) that commute with the transition
107	  isomorphisms on every overlap, there is a unique morphism
108	  \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of modules on \(X\) with
109	  \(\rho_i^{\mathcal{N}} \circ \iota_i^{*}\phi = \phi_i \circ \rho_i^{\mathcal{M}}\)
110	  for every \(i\) -- the descent of \(\mathrm{Hom}\) along the cover.
111	
112	  \emph{Construction.} A sheaf of modules on \(X\) is equivalent to a compatible
113	  family of sheaves on the members of an open cover of \(X\) together with
114	  identifications on the overlaps -- effective descent for the Zariski cover
115	  \(\{\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\}\). The charts \(U_i\), being the images of
116	  the glue datum's objects, form such a cover, with intersections
117	  \(U_i \cap U_j\) identified with the overlaps \(V_{ij}\) via the structural data
118	  of \(D\). Pulling the sections of an abstract \(\mathcal{M}\) back to each chart
119	  must reproduce \(\mathcal{M}_i\); the transition isomorphisms \(g_{ij}\),
120	  satisfying the cocycle conditions, are exactly the descent datum that makes the
121	  family \((\mathcal{M}_i)\) glue, so the descent produces \(\mathcal{M}\) with
122	  the asserted restriction isomorphisms \(\rho_i\). The morphism statement is the
123	  descent of morphisms for the same cover: a morphism on \(X\) is a family of
124	  morphisms on the charts agreeing on overlaps, and the compatibility of the
125	  \(\phi_i\) with \((g_{ij}, h_{ij})\) is precisely the agreement condition,
126	  giving the unique glued \(\phi\). This specialises the general descent of sheaves
127	  of modules to the concrete open cover supplied by a scheme glue datum,
128	  and is the form in which the Grassmannian's tautological bundle and quotient are
129	  built.
130	
131	  \emph{Realisation (construction path).} There is no turn-key gluing of sheaves
132	  of modules over a scheme glue datum available off the shelf; the construction
133	  below is original to this development, and it is the most involved declaration of
134	  the chapter -- expect a multi-step build assembled from the primitives named here,
135	  not an appeal to a single ready-made result. Concretely, instantiate \(D\) as the
136	  Grassmannian glue datum (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}), with glued scheme
137	  \(X = \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) and its canonical open cover by the charts
138	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\). The glued sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\) is built from
139	  the per-chart sheaves \(\mathcal{M}_i\) and the overlap transition isomorphisms
140	  \(g_{ij}\) satisfying the module cocycle conditions, and is pinned down by the
141	  restriction isomorphisms \(\rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim}
142	  \mathcal{M}_i\) compatible with the \(g_{ij}\). Restriction of a sheaf of modules
143	  to a chart is computed through the geometric restriction bridge of the
144	  development: for the open immersion \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\), the
145	  pullback \(\iota_i^{*}\) realises an equivalence between the sheaves of modules on
146	  \(X\) seen over the open \(U_i\) and the sheaves of modules on the open subscheme
147	  \(U_i\) itself -- the same open-immersion pullback restriction equivalence used to
148	  descend quasi-coherence along a basic open in the Quot-scheme construction. The
149	  transition isomorphisms \(g_{ij}\), forming a cocycle for this cover, constitute a
150	  descent datum; gluing the local sections then yields \(\mathcal{M}\) together with
151	  the restriction isomorphisms \(\rho_i\), where both the assembly and the
152	  characterisation up to unique isomorphism rest on the locality of sections of a
153	  sheaf -- a compatible family of sections over the cover, agreeing on the overlaps,
154	  extends to a unique global section.
155	
156	  The morphism-descent half is the same locality argument applied to homomorphisms:
157	  a family \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\) commuting with the transition
158	  isomorphisms over every overlap is a compatible family of local morphisms, and the
159	  uniqueness of gluing of sections promotes it to a unique global
160	  \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of modules on \(X\) restricting
161	  to each \(\phi_i\) through the \(\rho_i\).
162	\end{definition}
163	
164	\section{The tautological quotient on the charts}
165	\label{sec:grquot_chart}
166	
167	\emph{Infrastructure: scalar endomorphisms.} Realising a matrix of regular
168	functions as a morphism of free sheaves of modules has no off-the-shelf primitive
169	in Mathlib; it is assembled from two small project-local helpers that turn a global
170	function into a scalar endomorphism of the structure sheaf, after which the matrix
171	is reconstituted entry-by-entry over a finite biproduct.
172	
173	\begin{definition}[Global section of the unit module]
174	  \label{def:gr_globalUnitSection}
175	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.globalUnitSection}
176	  \uses{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
177	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) a global section of
178	  the structure sheaf. Viewing \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as the unit module
179	  \(\mathbf{1} \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}), the
180	  section \(a\) determines a global section of \(\mathbf{1}\): on each open
181	  \(V \subseteq X\) take the restriction \(a|_V \in \Gamma(V, \mathcal{O}_X)\), the
182	  family \((a|_V)_V\) being compatible with the restriction maps because
183	  restriction is functorial. This is the section of the unit sheaf attached to a
184	  global function.
185	\end{definition}

187	\begin{definition}[Scalar endomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)]
188	  \label{def:gr_scalarEnd}
189	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd}
190	  \uses{def:gr_globalUnitSection, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
191	  For \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) the \emph{scalar endomorphism}
192	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a) : \mathcal{O}_X \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is the
193	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear endomorphism of the unit module given by multiplication
194	  by \(a\). It is obtained from the global section of \cref{def:gr_globalUnitSection}
195	  under the canonical identification of endomorphisms of the unit module
196	  \(\mathbf{1}\) with its global sections, \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong
197	  \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\); concretely, on each open \(V\) it acts as
198	  multiplication by \(a|_V\). These scalar endomorphisms are the matrix-entry
199	  building blocks of the chart quotient.
200	\end{definition}
